compositions.name: OpenClaw-RL
compositions.intent: Train personalised LLM agents by turning live multi-turn conversations into fully-asynchronous RL training signals across terminal, GUI, software-engineering, and tool-call settings.
compositions.description: OpenClaw-RL is an Apache-2.0 reinforcement-learning framework from Gen-Verse that wraps a self-hosted model as an OpenAI-compatible API, intercepts live conversations through the OpenClaw plugin, and runs four async loops (agent serving, rollout collection, PRM/judge evaluation, policy training) that continuously optimise the policy without interrupting usage. Two paradigms are supported: Binary RL (GRPO with a Process Reward Model) and On-Policy Distillation (OPD via a judge model that emits textual hints), plus a Hybrid combination. Technical report on arXiv (2603.10165, 2026-03-10) reached #1 on HuggingFace Daily Papers.
primary_use_cases: personalising a self-hosted agent from a single user's conversational feedback
scaling RL training across terminal, GUI, SWE, and tool-call agent environments
continuously updating a deployed policy without taking the inference endpoint offline
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Four independent asynchronous loops (serving, rollout, judge, training) instead of a single synchronous agent loop. Conversation traffic flowing through an OpenAI-compatible wrapper feeds the rollout collector; the trainer updates the policy in the background while serving and judging continue concurrently.

key_concepts.name: OpenClaw plugin
key_concepts.summary: OpenAI-compatible wrapper around a self-hosted model that intercepts live multi-turn conversations and forwards them into the RL training pipeline.

key_concepts.name: PRM / Judge
key_concepts.summary: Process Reward Model and Judge component that scores each turn asynchronously, with majority voting for robustness.

key_concepts.name: Binary RL (GRPO)
key_concepts.summary: Per-turn scalar reward from the PRM combined with GRPO advantage estimation and a PPO-style clipped surrogate loss.

key_concepts.name: On-Policy Distillation (OPD)
key_concepts.summary: Judge extracts a textual hint from next-state hindsight; the token-level log-prob gap between teacher and student becomes a directional advantage signal.

key_concepts.name: Fully-asynchronous 4-component architecture
key_concepts.summary: Serving, rollout, judge, and training run as independent loops that do not block one another.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD USER[User conversation] --> PLUGIN[OpenClaw plugin
OpenAI-compatible wrapper] PLUGIN --> SERVE[Async loop: Agent serving] SERVE --> ROLLOUT[Async loop: Rollout collection] ROLLOUT --> JUDGE[Async loop: PRM / Judge
scores each turn + majority voting] JUDGE -->|Binary RL signal| TRAINER[Async loop: Policy training] JUDGE -->|OPD textual hints| TRAINER TRAINER -->|updated policy| SERVE PLUGIN -.->|tool calls| TOOLS[(Terminal / GUI / SWE / tool-call envs)] TOOLS -.->|next-state observation| ROLLOUT

members.note: An asynchronous Process Reward Model / judge model scores each turn; majority voting is used when needed for robust scoring. Judge-emitted hints also drive the OPD training signal.

evidence.title: OpenClaw-RL README — Features
evidence.quote: Runs PRM/judge evaluation asynchronously, with majority voting when needed for more robust scoring

evidence.title: OpenClaw-RL README — OPD method
evidence.quote: a judge model extracts a textual hint. This hint augments the original prompt to create an enhanced teacher

members.note: Evaluator (judge) and optimizer (trainer) are decoupled into independent async loops, so judging happens concurrently with new interactions and training runs in the background while serving continues.

evidence.title: OpenClaw-RL README — Fully Asynchronous 4-Component Architecture
evidence.quote: OpenClaw-RL decouples agent serving, rollout collection, PRM/judge evaluation, and policy training into independent async loops. None of them block one another

evidence.title: OpenClaw-RL Technical Report (arXiv 2603.10165)
evidence.quote: continuously optimises the policy in the background — all without interrupting usage

members.note: Live multi-turn conversation traffic, intercepted through the OpenClaw OpenAI-compatible plugin, is the event source feeding rollout collection and downstream training; nothing is batch-scheduled.

evidence.title: OpenClaw-RL README — TL;DR
evidence.quote: wraps your self-hosted model in OpenClaw as an OpenAI-compatible API, intercepts live multi-turn conversations, and continuously optimizes the policy in the background — all without interrupting your usage

members.note: Per-turn scalar reward from a Process Reward Model drives the Binary RL paradigm; GRPO advantage estimation plus a PPO-style clipped surrogate loss train the policy.

evidence.title: OpenClaw-RL README — Binary RL / PRM
evidence.quote: Per-turn scalar reward from the PRM combined with GRPO advantage estimation and a PPO-style clipped surrogate loss

members.note: Track 2 explicitly targets tool-call agents (alongside terminal, GUI, SWE) for real-world settings; supported as a training environment rather than a runtime feature.

evidence.title: OpenClaw-RL README — Roadmap / Track 2
evidence.quote: scalable RL implementations for general agent settings across terminal, GUI, SWE, and tool-call scenarios

alternatives.note: Same brand and openclaw.ai homepage; OpenClaw-RL is the RL training framework that wraps a model in the OpenClaw plugin so OpenClaw can intercept and learn from conversations.

compositions.name: Claude Agent SDK
compositions.intent: Embed Claude Code's autonomous agent loop — same tools, same context management — as a programmable library in Python or TypeScript so production agents can read files, run commands, edit code, and execute multi-step workflows.
compositions.description: The Claude Agent SDK is Anthropic's Python and TypeScript library for building agents that run Claude Code's loop inside your own process. It gives you the same tools, agent loop, and context management that power Claude Code, programmable in either language. The SDK exposes built-in tools (Read/Write/Edit/Bash/Glob/Grep/WebSearch/WebFetch/Agent/Skill), MCP servers (in-process and external), filesystem-defined Agents, Skills and Slash Commands under .claude/, hooks, and a canUseTool approval callback. It is the renamed successor of the Claude Code SDK; distinct from the lower-level Anthropic Client SDK (where you implement the tool loop) and from Managed Agents (Anthropic-hosted sandbox).
primary_use_cases: production coding agents that mirror the Claude Code shape
agents that need filesystem + bash + browser tools out of the box
agents with mid-run approval gates via canUseTool
agents using Anthropic skills and MCP servers
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Imperative query() / ClaudeSDKClient loop that wraps the Claude Code harness. Receive prompt → evaluate and respond → execute tools → repeat → return result. Turns continue until Claude produces output with no tool calls. Caller caps the loop with max_turns / maxTurns, max_budget_usd / maxBudgetUsd, effort, and permission_mode. Subagents spawn via the Agent tool, run with their own fresh context, and return a single final message. Sessions persist as JSONL under ~/.claude/projects/ and can be resumed, continued, or forked.

key_concepts.name: Agent loop
key_concepts.summary: Same execution loop that powers Claude Code; Receive → Evaluate → Execute tools → Repeat → Return result.

key_concepts.name: Subagents
key_concepts.summary: Separate agent instances the main agent can spawn for focused subtasks; their context starts fresh and intermediate tool calls stay inside the subagent.

key_concepts.name: Agent Skills
key_concepts.summary: Specialized capabilities packaged as SKILL.md files that Claude autonomously invokes when relevant; descriptions load at session start, full content only when used.

key_concepts.name: MCP servers
key_concepts.summary: Three transports — stdio, http/sse, and in-process SDK MCP servers; mcp_servers / mcpServers is a top-level option.

key_concepts.name: Permission modes + canUseTool
key_concepts.summary: Permission modes (default / dontAsk / acceptEdits / bypassPermissions / plan / auto-TS-only) layered with canUseTool callback that pauses execution until the host returns a decision.

key_concepts.name: Hooks
key_concepts.summary: Callback functions that run host code in response to agent events (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PermissionRequest, Stop).

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD USER[Host process
query() / ClaudeSDKClient] --> LOOP[Agent loop
Receive → Evaluate → Execute → Repeat] LOOP --> MODEL[Claude model] MODEL -->|tool calls| PERM{Permission check
hooks → deny → mode → allow → canUseTool} PERM -- approved --> TOOLS[(Built-in tools
Read/Write/Edit/Bash/Glob/Grep/
WebSearch/WebFetch/Agent/Skill)] PERM -- canUseTool pause --> HOST[Host approval callback] HOST -.->|resume with decision| PERM TOOLS --> OBS[Observation] OBS --> LOOP LOOP -.->|Agent tool spawn| SUB[Subagent
fresh context, no nested spawn] SUB -.->|final message only| LOOP LOOP --> SKILLS[(Agent Skills
.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md)] LOOP --> MCP[(MCP servers
stdio / HTTP / in-process)] LOOP --> SESS[(Sessions on disk
JSONL — resume/continue/fork)] LOOP -->|max_turns or max_budget_usd| OUT[Final result]

members.note: Sessions persist as JSONL on disk; resume by sessionId restores full context. continue (TS) / continue_conversation (Py) and fork_session also supported. Subagents themselves are resumable.

evidence.title: Sessions — resume
evidence.quote: Resume takes a specific session ID. You track the ID.

evidence.title: Sessions — disk persistence
evidence.quote: The SDK writes it to disk automatically so you can return to it later.

evidence.title: Subagents — resumable
evidence.quote: Subagents can be resumed to continue where they left off. Resumed subagents retain their full conversation history

members.note: Skills are markdown procedures loaded on demand from .claude/skills/ and ~/.claude/skills/; descriptions surface at session start, full content only when invoked.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — Skills
evidence.quote: Agent Skills extend Claude with specialized capabilities that Claude autonomously invokes when relevant. Skills are packaged as `SKILL.md` files

evidence.title: Agent SDK — Skills loading
evidence.quote: Skills are loaded from filesystem locations governed by `settingSources` (TypeScript) or `setting_sources` (Python)

members.note: canUseTool callback pauses the loop on tool requests; permission evaluation order is hooks → deny rules → permission mode → allow rules → canUseTool. Callbacks can stay pending indefinitely.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — User input / canUseTool
evidence.quote: Claude requests user input in two situations: when it needs permission to use a tool ... Both trigger your `canUseTool` callback, which pauses execution until you return a response.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — pause semantics
evidence.quote: The callback can stay pending indefinitely. Execution remains paused until your callback returns

members.note: ThinkingConfig and effort parameter expose Claude's reasoning behaviour. On Opus 4.7 manual extended thinking is no longer supported; adaptive thinking with effort replaces it. Downgraded from first-class because the SDK surfaces a model-level capability rather than implementing its own thinking pattern.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — TypeScript reference (thinking)
evidence.quote: thinking | ThinkingConfig | { type: 'adaptive' } for supported models | Controls Claude's thinking/reasoning behavior.

evidence.title: Agent loop — effort vs extended thinking
evidence.quote: effort trades latency and token cost for reasoning depth within each response. Extended thinking is a separate feature that produces visible chain-of-thought blocks in the output.

members.note: Three transports — stdio, HTTP, and in-process SDK MCP servers; mcp_servers / mcpServers top-level option.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — MCP
evidence.quote: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard for connecting AI agents to external tools and data sources.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — MCP transports
evidence.quote: MCP servers can run as local processes, connect over HTTP, or execute directly within your SDK application.

evidence.title: claude-agent-sdk-python README
evidence.quote: Custom tools are implemented in-process MCP servers that run directly within your Python application, eliminating the need for separate processes

members.note: Skill selection at session start via Pass-a-list or 'all'; description-first loading lets a large skill catalogue stay cheap.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — skill selection
evidence.quote: Pass a list of skill names, "all", or [] to control which are available in the session

evidence.title: Agent SDK — agent-loop / skills
evidence.quote: Skill descriptions | Session start, via setting sources | Short summaries; full content loads only when invoked

members.note: Caller caps the loop with max_turns / maxTurns (counts tool-use turns only) and max_budget_usd / maxBudgetUsd; exceeding emits a ResultMessage with error_max_turns or error_max_budget_usd.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — max_turns
evidence.quote: You can cap the loop with `max_turns` / `maxTurns`, which counts tool-use turns only.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — max_budget_usd
evidence.quote: You can also use `max_budget_usd` / `maxBudgetUsd` to cap turns based on a spend threshold.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — budget error subtypes
evidence.quote: When either limit is hit, the SDK returns a `ResultMessage` with a corresponding error subtype (`error_max_turns` or `error_max_budget_usd`).

members.note: outputFormat / output_format option accepts a JSON Schema (or Zod / Pydantic via .toJSONSchema / .model_json_schema); SDK validates output, re-prompts on mismatch, surfaces error_max_structured_output_retries on failure.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — TypeScript reference (outputFormat)
evidence.quote: outputFormat | { type: 'json_schema', schema: JSONSchema } | undefined | Define output format for agent results.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — Structured outputs (validation and re-prompt)
evidence.quote: Define a JSON Schema for the structure you need, and the SDK validates the output against it, re-prompting on mismatch.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — Structured outputs (retry-limit error)
evidence.quote: error_max_structured_output_retries | Agent couldn't produce valid output after multiple attempts

evidence.title: Agent SDK — Structured outputs (Zod/Pydantic)
evidence.quote: For full type safety, use Zod (TypeScript) or Pydantic (Python) to define your schema and get strongly-typed objects back.

members.note: Each subagent runs in its own fresh conversation; intermediate tool calls/results stay inside and only the final message returns to the parent. Subagents cannot spawn subagents.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — Subagents (fresh context)
evidence.quote: Each subagent runs in its own fresh conversation. Intermediate tool calls and results stay inside the subagent; only its final message returns to the parent.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — Subagents (parent → child channel)
evidence.quote: A subagent's context window starts fresh (no parent conversation) but isn't empty. The only channel from parent to subagent is the Agent tool's prompt string

evidence.title: Agent SDK — Subagents (no nesting)
evidence.quote: Subagents cannot spawn their own subagents.

members.note: Prompt caching of the running context (system prompt, tool definitions, CLAUDE.md, accumulated history) is automatic; this is not deduplicated caching keyed on individual tool calls.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — Cost tracking (automatic prompt caching)
evidence.quote: The Agent SDK automatically uses prompt caching to reduce costs on repeated content. You do not need to configure caching yourself.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — agent-loop / cached content
evidence.quote: Content that stays the same across turns (system prompt, tool definitions, CLAUDE.md) is automatically prompt cached

members.note: Built-in tools (Read/Write/Edit/Bash/Glob/Grep/WebSearch/WebFetch/Agent/Skill/AskUserQuestion/Monitor) + custom tools via @tool / create_sdk_mcp_server + external MCP servers.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — built-in tools
evidence.quote: Your agent can read files, run commands, and search codebases out of the box. Key tools include: Read ... Write ... Edit ... Bash ... Monitor ... Glob ... Grep ... WebSearch ... WebFetch ... AskUserQuestion

evidence.title: Agent SDK — tools rationale
evidence.quote: Tools give your agent the ability to take action. Without tools, Claude can only respond with text. With tools, Claude can read files, run commands, search code, and interact with external services.

anti_patterns_avoided.note: Hooks + deny rules + canUseTool allow explicit interception of every tool invocation before execution; the SDK warns that allowed_tools does not constrain bypassPermissions and that an allow-list paired with dontAsk is the safer pattern.

evidence.title: Agent SDK — permissions warning
evidence.quote: allowed_tools does not constrain bypassPermissions

alternatives.note: Same capabilities, different interface — Claude Code is the CLI/IDE product; Claude Agent SDK is the library that exposes the same loop.
Cross-vendor SDK in the same agent-SDK category.
Same category, Google equivalent.
TypeScript-first competitor in the same category.
Same category, different vendor.

compositions.name: Agent Development Kit (ADK)
compositions.intent: Provide a code-first, model-agnostic Python and Java framework for composing LLM agents with deterministic workflow agents into multi-agent applications that can be evaluated and deployed at enterprise scale.
compositions.description: Agent Development Kit (ADK) is Google's open-source, Apache-2.0 framework for building, evaluating, and deploying AI agents from a single LlmAgent up to multi-agent workflows. The 2.0 release adds a graph-based Workflow Runtime that interleaves deterministic execution flows with LLM reasoning, plus a Task API for structured agent-to-agent delegation. Core abstractions include LlmAgent (instructions + model + tools), the workflow agents Sequential / Parallel / Loop, sub_agents trees, MCP tool integration via McpToolset, an evaluation harness (AgentEvaluator, eval sets, tool-trajectory and response-match criteria), and first-class deployment to Cloud Run, GKE, and Vertex AI Agent Runtime.
primary_use_cases: production multi-agent workflows on Google Cloud (Agent Runtime, Cloud Run, GKE)
deterministic orchestration via SequentialAgent / ParallelAgent / LoopAgent
LLM-coordinated dynamic routing across sub-agents
evaluation-driven agent development with eval sets and trajectory matching
MCP-tool integration into Gemini and other LLMs
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Hierarchical agent tree with a graph-based Workflow Runtime. An LlmAgent runs the standard reason-act-observe loop over its tools; workflow agents (Sequential / Parallel / Loop) compose sub_agents with fixed execution logic; in collaborative workflows an LlmAgent coordinator dynamically routes work across sub_agents. ADK 2.0 adds a graph-based execution engine that interleaves deterministic nodes with LLM agents inside one run.

key_concepts.name: LlmAgent
key_concepts.summary: Self-contained execution unit: model + instructions + tools. The core building block, often aliased simply as Agent.

key_concepts.name: Workflow Agents (Sequential / Parallel / Loop)
key_concepts.summary: Specialised agents that control execution flow over sub-agents with predefined, deterministic logic.

key_concepts.name: Collaborative workflow
key_concepts.summary: Single LlmAgent acts as dynamic coordinator over a set of sub_agents.

key_concepts.name: McpToolset
key_concepts.summary: Bridges an MCP server into ADK: connects, lists tools, converts them to ADK BaseTool instances, manages shutdown.

key_concepts.name: output_schema / output_key
key_concepts.summary: output_schema enforces a JSON shape on the final response; output_key auto-saves the response to session state.

key_concepts.name: AgentEvaluator + eval sets
key_concepts.summary: Evaluation harness comparing actual trajectories to expected ones via tool_trajectory_avg_score, response_match_score, rubric_based_final_response_quality_v1, and similar criteria.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD HOST[Host app / runner] --> ROOT[Root LlmAgent
instructions + model + tools] ROOT -->|tool calls| TOOLS[(Function / OpenAPI / Built-in tools)] ROOT -->|MCP| MCP[McpToolset
stdio + SSE] ROOT -->|sub_agents tree| WF{Workflow Agent} WF --> SEQ[SequentialAgent] WF --> PAR[ParallelAgent] WF --> LOOP[LoopAgent] WF --> COORD[Collaborative coordinator
dynamic routing] SEQ --> CHILD[(sub_agents)] PAR --> CHILD LOOP --> CHILD COORD --> CHILD ROOT -.->|output_schema| OBJ[Structured JSON] ROOT -.->|output_key| STATE[(Session state)] HOST -.->|AgentEvaluator + eval sets| EVAL[(Trajectory + response-match criteria)]

members.note: Tools (function tools, OpenAPI tools, MCP tools, built-in tools) attach to an LlmAgent via its tools list and the LLM invokes them through the standard reason-act-observe loop.

evidence.title: ADK — LlmAgent tools
evidence.quote: Tools give your `LlmAgent` capabilities beyond the LLM's built-in knowledge or reasoning

evidence.title: ADK — agent definition
evidence.quote: The basic components of an **_Agent_** are an artificial intelligence (AI) model, task instructions, and optionally, a set of tools to be used by the agent.

members.note: McpToolset connects to an MCP server, lists tools, converts them to ADK BaseTool instances and manages graceful shutdown; both StdioServerParameters and SSE-style connections are supported.

evidence.title: ADK — MCP tools
evidence.quote: When you include an McpToolset instance in your agent's tools list, it automatically handles the interaction with the specified MCP server.

evidence.title: ADK — MCP tool discovery
evidence.quote: Once connected, McpToolset queries the MCP server for its available tools (via the list_tools MCP method). It then converts the schemas of these discovered MCP tools into ADK-compatible BaseTool instances.

members.note: LlmAgent.output_schema constrains the final response to a JSON shape; output_key writes the response into session state for downstream agents.

evidence.title: ADK — LlmAgent output_schema
evidence.quote: Define a schema representing the desired output structure. If set, the agent's final response must be JSON

evidence.title: ADK — LlmAgent output_key
evidence.quote: If set, the text content of the agent's final response will be automatically saved to the session's state

members.note: Collaborative workflow places one LlmAgent in a dynamic coordinator role over a set of sub_agents that automatically return to the coordinator after task completion; orchestration is LLM-driven rather than predefined.

evidence.title: ADK — Workflows (collaborative)
evidence.quote: This workflow type allows a single agent to act in a dynamic coordinator role to accomplish tasks with a set of specified sub-agents

evidence.title: ADK — Collaborative coordinator
evidence.quote: a coordinator agent handles delegation of tasks to one or more subagents

members.note: Workflow agents (Sequential / Parallel / Loop) execute sub-agents under fixed deterministic logic, yielding orchestrator-worker shape.

evidence.title: ADK — Workflow agents
evidence.quote: Specialized agents that control the execution flow of one or more sub-agents.

evidence.title: ADK — Workflow agents (deterministic)
evidence.quote: Template workflow agents operate based on predefined logic...This approach results in deterministic and predictable execution patterns.

evidence.title: ADK — Workflow agents (composition)
evidence.quote: These pre-built workflows are extended from **_BaseAgent_** and provide fixed execution logic structures including sequences, loops, and parallel execution

members.note: ADK 2.0 ships a Task API for structured agent-to-agent delegation; combined with sub_agents trees and the collaborative coordinator this realises the handoff pattern.

evidence.title: google/adk-python README — Task API
evidence.quote: Task API: Structured agent-to-agent delegation with multi-turn task mode

evidence.title: ADK — Workflows scope
evidence.quote: Agent Development Kit (ADK) supports building sophisticated agent applications by composing multiple agents and executable nodes into _agent workflows_

members.note: AgentEvaluator with eval sets compares actual trajectories to expected ones; multiple built-in criteria (tool_trajectory_avg_score, response_match_score, rubric_based_final_response_quality_v1) drive an evaluation -> iteration cycle.

evidence.title: ADK — Evaluate (trajectory)
evidence.quote: The trajectory is just a list of steps the agent took before it returned to the user. We can compare that against the list of steps we expect the agent to have taken.

evidence.title: ADK — Evaluate (built-in criteria)
evidence.quote: ADK provides several built-in criteria for evaluating agent performance, ranging from tool trajectory matching to LLM-based response quality assessment.

members.note: sub_agents trees give an LlmAgent named children it can delegate to with automatic return-to-parent behaviour after each task; ADK does not document a hard context-isolation boundary at the level Anthropic's Agent tool does, so isolation is structural rather than enforced.

evidence.title: ADK — Workflows (composition)
evidence.quote: Workflows: multi-agent, multi-node applications

evidence.title: ADK — Collaborative (subagents return to parent)
evidence.quote: subagents defined to handle specific tasks, and automatic return to the parent after completing a task

members.note: ADK ships the vertex_ai_rag_retrieval tool that lets an LlmAgent perform private-data retrieval via Vertex AI Knowledge Engine; agentic RAG is a first-party integration but not a top-level abstraction.

evidence.title: ADK — LlmAgent (tool surface)
evidence.quote: Tools give your `LlmAgent` capabilities beyond the LLM's built-in knowledge or reasoning

evidence.title: ADK — vertex_ai_rag_retrieval
evidence.quote: The `vertex_ai_rag_retrieval` tool allows the agent to perform private data retrieval using Knowledge Engine.

alternatives.note: Same agent-sdk category; ADK is Python/Java + Google-Cloud-first, Claude Agent SDK is Anthropic-only with built-in filesystem tools.
Cross-vendor competitor; OpenAI is handoff-centric, ADK is workflow-agent-centric with explicit deterministic orchestration.
TypeScript-first competitor in the same category.

references.title: google/adk-python README
references.quote: An open-source, code-first Python framework for building, evaluating, and deploying sophisticated AI agents with flexibility and control.

references.title: google/adk-python README — license
references.quote: This project is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License

compositions.name: Instructor
compositions.language: Python, TypeScript, Go, Ruby, Elixir, Rust
compositions.intent: Get reliable, type-safe structured data from any LLM by patching the provider client to accept a Pydantic response_model, validate the response, and retry with validation feedback when the model violates the schema.
compositions.description: Instructor is the MIT-licensed library that turns Pydantic models into the contract between application code and an LLM. It patches each supported provider's chat-completion client to add response_model, max_retries, and context parameters, then wraps the call: when validation fails, the library reasks the model with the validation error attached so the next attempt converges on a valid object. Modes (TOOLS, JSON, MD_JSON, FUNCTIONS) select the underlying provider mechanism; from_provider exposes a unified entry point across 15+ providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral, Cohere, Ollama, DeepSeek, Groq, and more). Instructor is positioned as an extraction library, not an agent framework: 'Instructor for extraction, PydanticAI for agents.'
primary_use_cases: extracting typed objects from LLM responses
schema-validated tool-call arguments before execution
multi-provider structured-output code that does not change when swapping models
self-correcting JSON output via validation-feedback retries
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Single-call extraction with internal retry, not an agent loop. The patched create() runs the request, parses the response into the response_model, validates it, and on ValidationError reasks the model up to max_retries times. There is no multi-step tool loop, no handoff, no session memory; agentic behaviour requires composing Instructor inside a higher-level framework.

key_concepts.name: Patching
key_concepts.summary: Adds response_model, max_retries, and context to the provider's create() method without changing original client code.

key_concepts.name: response_model
key_concepts.summary: Pydantic BaseModel that defines the expected output shape; docstrings and field annotations feed the prompt.

key_concepts.name: Modes (TOOLS / JSON / MD_JSON / FUNCTIONS)
key_concepts.summary: Selects which provider-side mechanism Instructor uses to coerce the model into the schema. OpenAI / Anthropic / Google default to TOOLS.

key_concepts.name: max_retries with validation feedback
key_concepts.summary: On Pydantic ValidationError Instructor retries with the validation error fed back to the model.

key_concepts.name: from_provider
key_concepts.summary: Unified entry point across all supported providers; recommended over manual patching.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD APP[Application] --> CLIENT[Patched provider client
via patch() or from_provider()] CLIENT --> CREATE[create(response_model=Model, max_retries=N)] CREATE -->|TOOLS / JSON / MD_JSON| LLM[Provider LLM] LLM --> RESP[Raw response] RESP --> VAL{Pydantic validate} VAL -- ok --> OUT[Typed Pydantic instance] VAL -- ValidationError --> REASK[Reask with error feedback] REASK --> CREATE

members.note: Core purpose: response_model is a Pydantic class that defines the LLM output shape, drives the prompt via docstrings and field annotations, and is used to validate the response.

evidence.title: Instructor homepage — tagline
evidence.quote: Extract structured data from any LLM with type safety, validation, and automatic retries.

evidence.title: Instructor concepts — models
evidence.quote: Define LLM output schemas using `pydantic.BaseModel`.

evidence.title: Instructor concepts — patching
evidence.quote: New parameters: `response_model`, `max_retries`, and `context` to completion methods

members.note: On Pydantic ValidationError Instructor reasks the model with the validation feedback attached, up to max_retries times; this is the self-refine loop applied to structured-output extraction.

evidence.title: Instructor concepts — patching (validation)
evidence.quote: Validates responses: Checks LLM outputs against your Pydantic model. Handles retries: Retries with validation feedback if needed

evidence.title: Instructor concepts — retrying
evidence.quote: Failed attempts are automatically propagated to reask handlers, enabling contextual error messages and progressive corrections.

members.note: Instructor does not implement an agent tool loop; it uses the provider's tool-calling API (TOOLS mode) as the transport for structured output. Building a true tool-loop agent requires wrapping Instructor inside another framework.

evidence.title: Instructor concepts — patching modes
evidence.quote: Tool Calling (TOOLS): Uses the provider's function/tool calling API

evidence.title: Instructor homepage — positioning
evidence.quote: Instructor for extraction, PydanticAI for agents. Instructor shines when you need fast, schema-first extraction without extra agents.

members.note: from_provider exposes a single API surface across 15+ providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral, Cohere, Ollama, DeepSeek, Groq, ...) so the same response_model code works across vendors.

evidence.title: Instructor homepage — providers
evidence.quote: works seamlessly with 15+ popular LLM providers

evidence.title: Instructor concepts — from_provider
evidence.quote: Use `from_provider` instead of manual patching. It works the same way across all providers.

members.note: Its llm_validator plugs an LLM-based business-rule check into the same Pydantic model and re-asks the model on failure, so a returned result is gated on validity rather than merely on a clean call.

evidence.title: Enhancing AI Validations with Pydantic's Framework - Instructor
evidence.quote: LLM-based validation can also be plugged into the same Pydantic model. Here, if the answer attribute contains content that violates the rule 'don't say objectionable things,' Pydantic will raise a validation error.

alternatives.note: Vercel's generateObject covers the structured-output slice in TypeScript with Zod schemas.
OpenAI Agents SDK uses Pydantic output_type for structured agent results; Instructor is the standalone extraction library.

references.title: Instructor homepage
references.quote: Extract structured data from any LLM with type safety, validation, and automatic retries.

references.title: instructor-ai/instructor
references.quote: Get reliable JSON from any LLM. Built on Pydantic for validation, type safety, and IDE support.

compositions.name: OpenAI Agents SDK
compositions.intent: Provide a lightweight, production-ready Python and TypeScript framework for building multi-agent workflows around four primitives: Agents, handoffs, guardrails, and sessions, with built-in tracing for the entire run.
compositions.description: The OpenAI Agents SDK is the MIT-licensed successor to Swarm, shipped in March 2025. Its agent loop is small and explicit: Runner.run(agent, input) calls the LLM, executes any tool calls, follows any handoffs, and re-runs until the agent emits a final_output or max_turns is exceeded (MaxTurnsExceeded). Agents are LLMs configured with instructions, tools, optional output_type, handoffs, and guardrails. Handoffs are exposed to the model as transfer_to_<agent> tools with optional input_filter to reshape the conversation history. MCP support ships with three transports (Stdio, SSE, Streamable HTTP). SQLiteSession is the default session-memory backing store and supports run resumption across pauses.
primary_use_cases: multi-agent workflows where specialised agents hand off control
agents that call MCP servers alongside Python function tools
agents with input/output guardrails and Pydantic-typed outputs
production agents that need built-in tracing and session memory
TypeScript agents sharing the same primitive set as the Python SDK
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Runner.run loop: call LLM -> if final_output, return; if handoff, swap current agent and re-run; if tool calls, execute and append results then re-run. Loop is capped by max_turns (None disables). Sessions automatically prepend prior history before each run and store new items afterwards.

key_concepts.name: Agent
key_concepts.summary: LLM configured with instructions, tools, optional output_type, handoffs, guardrails.

key_concepts.name: Runner
key_concepts.summary: Runs the agent loop via Runner.run / run_sync / run_streamed; capped by max_turns, raises MaxTurnsExceeded.

key_concepts.name: Handoffs
key_concepts.summary: Sub-agent delegation exposed as transfer_to_<agent> tools; handoff() customises tool name, description, on_handoff callback, input_filter.

key_concepts.name: Guardrails
key_concepts.summary: Input guardrails run on the first agent's input; output guardrails on the last agent's final output. Input guardrails can run in parallel or block.

key_concepts.name: Sessions
key_concepts.summary: Built-in session memory (SQLiteSession default) that prepends history before each run and stores new items afterwards; supports resumption after approval pauses.

key_concepts.name: MCP transports
key_concepts.summary: MCPServerStdio (subprocess), MCPServerSse (HTTP+SSE), MCPServerStreamableHttp (self-managed connection); tools exposed identically to function tools.

key_concepts.name: output_type
key_concepts.summary: Optional Pydantic / typed output declaration that switches the model into structured-outputs mode.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD CALLER[Caller] --> RUN[Runner.run / run_sync / run_streamed] RUN --> AGENT[current_agent: Agent
instructions + tools + output_type] AGENT --> LLM[LLM call] LLM --> DEC{output?} DEC -- final_output --> OUT[RunResult] DEC -- tool calls --> TOOLS[(Function tools
+ MCP tools)] TOOLS --> RUN DEC -- transfer_to_X --> RUN RUN -.->|max_turns exceeded| ERR[MaxTurnsExceeded] RUN -.->|input guardrails| GIN[Input guardrails
first agent only] OUT -.->|output guardrails| GOUT[Output guardrails
last agent only] RUN -.->|prepend / append| SESS[(SQLiteSession or custom)] RUN -.->|tracing| TRACE[(Built-in tracing)] TOOLS -.->|MCPServerStdio
MCPServerSse
MCPServerStreamableHttp| MCP[(MCP servers)]

members.note: Agent.tools accepts function tools (auto-schemaed from Python signatures) and MCP-exposed tools; the runner's loop executes tool calls and appends results before re-running.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — Agent class
evidence.quote: An agent is a large language model (LLM) configured with instructions, tools, and optional runtime behavior such as handoffs, guardrails, and structured outputs.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — Runner loop
evidence.quote: If the LLM produces tool calls, we run those tool calls, append the results, and re-run the loop.

evidence.title: openai/openai-agents-python README
evidence.quote: Agents: LLMs configured with instructions, tools, guardrails, and handoffs

members.note: Handoffs are surfaced to the LLM as transfer_to_<agent> tools; handoff() exposes tool_name_override, on_handoff, input_type, input_filter, is_enabled. The runner swaps current_agent on handoff and continues the same run.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — Handoffs as tools
evidence.quote: Handoffs are represented as tools to the LLM. So if there's a handoff to an agent named `Refund Agent`, the tool would be called `transfer_to_refund_agent`.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — Handoff effect
evidence.quote: If the LLM does a handoff, we update the current agent and input, and re-run the loop.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — Handoff delegation
evidence.quote: Handoffs are sub‑agents the agent can delegate to. When a handoff occurs, the delegated agent receives the conversation history and takes over.

members.note: Three transports ship in the box: MCPServerStdio for subprocess servers, MCPServerSse for HTTP+SSE, MCPServerStreamableHttp for self-managed connections. MCP tools appear identically to function tools.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — MCP stdio
evidence.quote: For MCP servers that run as local subprocesses, use MCPServerStdio.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — MCP SSE
evidence.quote: If the MCP server implements the HTTP with SSE transport, instantiate MCPServerSse.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — MCP streamable HTTP
evidence.quote: When you want to manage the network connection yourself, use MCPServerStreamableHttp.

members.note: max_turns argument on Runner.run / run_sync / run_streamed caps the loop; exceeding it raises MaxTurnsExceeded. Pass None to disable.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — max_turns
evidence.quote: If we exceed the `max_turns` passed, we raise a `MaxTurnsExceeded` exception. Pass `max_turns=None` to disable this turn limit.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — MaxTurnsExceeded source
evidence.quote: MaxTurnsExceeded: This exception is raised when the agent's run exceeds the `max_turns` limit passed to the `Runner.run`, `Runner.run_sync`, or `Runner.run_streamed` methods.

members.note: Agent.output_type accepts a Pydantic / typed return; when set the SDK switches the model into structured-outputs mode.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — output_type
evidence.quote: By default, agents produce plain text (i.e. `str`) outputs. If you want the agent to produce a particular type of output, you can use the `output_type` parameter.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — structured outputs mode
evidence.quote: When you pass an `output_type`, that tells the model to use structured outputs instead of regular plain text responses.

members.note: Sessions persist conversation history; the runner prepends prior items before each run and stores new items afterwards. Runs that pause for approval can resume on the same session instance.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — Sessions overview
evidence.quote: The Agents SDK provides built-in session memory to automatically maintain conversation history across multiple agent runs

evidence.title: Agents SDK — Sessions resume
evidence.quote: If a run pauses for approval, resume it with the same session instance (or another session instance that points at the same backing store) so the resumed turn continues the same stored conversation history.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — SQLiteSession
evidence.quote: The default, lightweight session implementation using SQLite

members.note: Two supported supervisor shapes are documented: (a) a manager agent that keeps the conversation and calls specialists through Agent.as_tool, and (b) decentralised peer handoff via transfer_to_*.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — decentralised handoff
evidence.quote: Peer agents hand off control to a specialized agent that takes over the conversation. This is decentralized.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — manager agent (agents as tools)
evidence.quote: A manager agent keeps control of the conversation and calls specialist agents through `Agent.as_tool()`.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — triage agent as router
evidence.quote: A triage agent routes the conversation to a specialist, and that specialist becomes the active agent for the rest of the turn.

members.note: Handoffs transfer control rather than spawn an isolated subagent; the delegated agent receives the conversation history (optionally reshaped via input_filter). Nested handoffs are an opt-in beta that collapses prior transcript into a single assistant summary message.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — handoff receives history
evidence.quote: When a handoff occurs, the delegated agent receives the conversation history and takes over.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — nested handoff
evidence.quote: Nested handoffs are available as an opt-in beta... the runner collapses the prior transcript into a single assistant summary message.

members.note: Input guardrails run only for the first agent in the chain; output guardrails run only for the agent producing the final output. Input guardrails can run in parallel with the agent (default) or block before it starts.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — input guardrails
evidence.quote: Input guardrails run on the initial user input

evidence.title: Agents SDK — output guardrails
evidence.quote: Output guardrails run on the final agent output

evidence.title: Agents SDK — guardrail execution modes
evidence.quote: Parallel execution (default, `run_in_parallel=True`): The guardrail runs concurrently with the agent's execution

members.note: The Runner loop has the reason-act-observe shape (LLM produces output, either final or tool calls, which are executed and fed back). Upstream docs do not use the term 'ReAct'; downgraded from first-class to supported because the pattern is implemented but not named.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — loop steps
evidence.quote: The runner then runs a loop: 1. We call the LLM for the current agent, with the current input. 2. The LLM produces its output.

evidence.title: Agents SDK — loop termination
evidence.quote: If the LLM returns a `final_output`, the loop ends and we return the result.

members.note: Tools marked needsApproval record an approval interruption instead of executing, pausing the run before side effects such as edits, shell commands, or sensitive MCP actions; reversibility is a recommended risk axis rather than a built-in classifier.

evidence.title: Guardrails and human review | OpenAI API
evidence.quote: The run records an approval interruption instead of executing the tool. The result returns `interruptions` plus a resumable `state`. Your application approves or rejects the pending items.

alternatives.note: Production-ready evolution of Swarm; same primitive set (agents + handoffs) plus guardrails, sessions, tracing, MCP.
Same agent-sdk category, different vendor; Anthropic's SDK is filesystem-tool-first, OpenAI's is handoff-first.
Google's competitor in the agent-sdk category.
TypeScript-first competitor in the same category.
Peer minimal agent SDK with a model-driven loop.
OpenAI Agents SDK uses Pydantic output_type for structured agent results; Instructor is the standalone extraction library.

references.title: OpenAI Agents SDK — docs root
references.quote: The OpenAI Agents SDK enables you to build agentic AI apps in a lightweight, easy-to-use package with very few abstractions.

references.title: openai/openai-agents-python
references.quote: A lightweight, powerful framework for multi-agent workflows

compositions.name: OpenAI Swarm
compositions.intent: Explore an ergonomic, minimal model of multi-agent orchestration where agents are functions, control transfer is just returning another Agent from a tool, and a single client.run() drives the whole loop.
compositions.description: OpenAI Swarm is an MIT-licensed educational framework (not for production) from the OpenAI Solution team. Its API has two primitives: an Agent (name, model, instructions, functions, tool_choice) and a handoff that happens when a function returns another Agent. context_variables flow through every call and can be updated via a Result return. The library is no longer recommended for new code; the README states 'Swarm is now replaced by the OpenAI Agents SDK, which is a production-ready evolution of Swarm. We recommend migrating to the Agents SDK for all production use cases.'
primary_use_cases: studying minimal multi-agent orchestration as a teaching reference
small experimental routines where agents transfer control by returning each other
porting designs into the OpenAI Agents SDK
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Single client.run() loop, analogous to chat.completions.create(). On each turn the current Agent is called, any tool functions execute; if a function returns an Agent, the runner swaps current_agent and continues; if it returns a Result, context_variables update. The loop terminates when the agent produces a plain message with no further handoff or tool call.

key_concepts.name: Agent
key_concepts.summary: Bundle of name, model, instructions (string or callable), functions, tool_choice. The unit of behaviour.

key_concepts.name: Handoff via function return
key_concepts.summary: Returning another Agent from a function transfers control to that Agent for the next turn.

key_concepts.name: context_variables
key_concepts.summary: Dict-shaped state passed into instructions and functions; persists across handoffs and can be updated via a Result return.

key_concepts.name: client.run()
key_concepts.summary: Single-entry runner that drives the loop, analogous to chat.completions.create.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD CALLER[Caller] --> RUN[client.run
messages + context_variables + current_agent] RUN --> AGENT[current_agent
name + model + instructions + functions] AGENT --> LLM[LLM call] LLM --> DEC{output} DEC -- plain message --> OUT[Final response] DEC -- function call --> FN[Run function] FN -- returns str --> RUN FN -- returns Agent --> SWAP[Swap current_agent] SWAP --> RUN FN -- returns Result --> CTX[Update context_variables] CTX --> RUN

members.note: Handoff is the framework's defining primitive: an Agent transfers control by being returned from a function call. There is no router, no graph, no scheduler.

evidence.title: Swarm README — primitives
evidence.quote: An `Agent` encompasses `instructions` and `tools`, and can at any point choose to hand off a conversation to another `Agent`.

evidence.title: Swarm README — handoff via return
evidence.quote: An `Agent` can hand off to another `Agent` by returning it in a `function`.

members.note: Functions are Python callables on the Agent; type hints and docstrings generate the schema. Functions can also read context_variables and return either a string result or a new Agent (handoff) or a Result (updates context).

evidence.title: Swarm README — Agent fields
evidence.quote: An `Agent` encompasses `instructions` and `tools`

evidence.title: Swarm README — client.run
evidence.quote: analogous to the `chat.completions.create()` function in the Chat Completions API

members.note: There is no dedicated supervisor primitive: any Agent can become the supervisor by routing other Agents through its functions. The triage_agent example demonstrates a supervisor by handoff. The pattern is achievable, not first-class. Downgraded from first-class.

evidence.title: Swarm README — coordination scope
evidence.quote: Swarm focuses on making agent **coordination** and **execution** lightweight, highly controllable, and easily testable.

evidence.title: Swarm triage_agent example
evidence.quote: This example is a Swarm containing a triage agent, which takes in user inputs and chooses whether to respond directly, or triage the request to a sales or refunds agent.

members.note: Swarm has no Pydantic output_type or response_model; the README documents only two primitive abstractions (Agents and handoffs), and structured output is only available through tool/function call signatures or by the application parsing the final string.

evidence.title: Swarm README — minimal primitives
evidence.quote: Two primitive abstractions: Agents and handoffs

evidence.title: Swarm README — Agent fields (no output_type)
evidence.quote: An `Agent` encompasses `instructions` and `tools`

alternatives.note: Direct successor; production-ready evolution of the same primitive set.
Same category, different vendor.
Handoff-based swarm pattern.
Both are code-first agent frameworks; Swarm uses agent-as-tool handoffs, TaskWeaver uses stateful Python sessions.

references.title: openai/swarm README
references.quote: Educational framework exploring ergonomic, lightweight multi-agent orchestration. Managed by OpenAI Solution team.

references.title: openai/swarm README — deprecation
references.quote: Swarm is now replaced by the OpenAI Agents SDK, which is a production-ready evolution of Swarm.

references.title: openai/swarm README — migration recommendation
references.quote: We recommend migrating to the Agents SDK for all production use cases.

compositions.name: Vercel AI SDK
compositions.intent: Provide a free, TypeScript-first toolkit that standardises calls to any model provider with a single API for text generation, structured-object generation, streaming UIs, tool-calling, and multi-step agent loops in TypeScript/JavaScript runtimes including browser, Node.js, and Vercel Edge.
compositions.description: The Vercel AI SDK is the Apache-2.0 TypeScript toolkit from Vercel split into AI SDK Core ('A unified API for generating text, structured objects, tool calls, and building agents with LLMs') and AI SDK UI ('A set of framework-agnostic hooks for quickly building chat and generative user interface'). Core exposes generateText, streamText, and the structured-output siblings generateObject / streamObject driven by Zod, Valibot, or JSON Schema. The agent loop is opt-in via stopWhen (default stepCountIs(20)). UI layer ships useChat and useCompletion hooks for React (plus Vue/Svelte equivalents). Designed for edge runtimes alongside Node.js.
primary_use_cases: TypeScript agents over multiple LLM providers behind one API
streaming chat UIs via useChat / streamText
Zod-typed structured outputs
MCP tool integration in TypeScript
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Imperative TypeScript loop. Caller invokes generateText({ model, tools, stopWhen, ... }) or streamText(...). In each step the model either generates text (loop ends) or calls a tool (executed in-process, observation feeds back in for another generation). Loop terminates when stopWhen is satisfied; default cap stepCountIs(20). No graph DSL, no class hierarchy — just async functions, plain objects, and AbortSignal.

key_concepts.name: generateText / streamText
key_concepts.summary: Core agent-loop entry points; streamText returns a token stream.

key_concepts.name: tools
key_concepts.summary: A tool consists of three properties: description, inputSchema (Zod or JSON schema), and execute.

key_concepts.name: stopWhen / stepCountIs
key_concepts.summary: Loop-termination predicate; stepCountIs(20) is the default safety cap.

key_concepts.name: generateObject + Zod
key_concepts.summary: Structured-output generation with schema validation.

key_concepts.name: Providers (language model spec)
key_concepts.summary: Standardised interface abstracting provider differences (OpenAI / Anthropic / Google / xAI / Bedrock / Vertex / Gateway).

key_concepts.name: useChat hook
key_concepts.summary: React UI hook for conversational interfaces (framework-agnostic equivalents for Vue/Svelte).

key_concepts.name: AbortSignal / onAbort
key_concepts.summary: Cancellation lifecycle — onAbort fires when streams are aborted via AbortSignal; onFinish is skipped.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD
  CALLER[Caller / Route handler] --> GT[generateText / streamText]
  GT -->|loop until stopWhen| MODEL[LLM via provider]
  MODEL --> DEC{tool call?}
  DEC -- yes --> TOOL[tool.execute]
  TOOL --> GT
  DEC -- no --> OUT[Final text or object]
  GT -.->|tools| TOOLS[(tools: description+inputSchema+execute)]
  GT -.->|MCP| MCP[MCP client tools]
  GT -.->|stopWhen| BUDGET[stepCountIs / hasToolCall]
  GT -.->|AbortSignal| ABRT[AbortController → onAbort]
  GT -.->|providerMetadata| SRC[(sources stream)]
  GT -.->|schema| OBJ[generateObject / Output.object]
  OUT --> UI[useChat / useCompletion]
  UI -.->|stop()| ABRT

members.note: Sources surface as typed source stream-parts (sourceType: 'url'); each entry has id, url, title, providerMetadata. Upstream uses the word 'sources' rather than 'citations'.

evidence.title: AI SDK — sources from generateText
evidence.quote: Some providers such as Perplexity and Google Generative AI include sources in the response.

evidence.title: AI SDK — url source shape
evidence.quote: Each `url` source contains the following properties: `id`, `url`, `title`, and `providerMetadata`.

evidence.title: AI SDK — sources via fullStream
evidence.quote: When you use `streamText`, you can access the sources using the `fullStream` property.

members.note: Three transports — HTTP (recommended), SSE, stdio. client.tools() adapts MCP tool definitions to AI SDK tool definitions.

evidence.title: AI SDK — MCP overview
evidence.quote: The AI SDK supports connecting to Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers to access their tools. MCP enables your AI applications to discover and use tools across various services through a standardized interface.

evidence.title: AI SDK — MCP transports
evidence.quote: Create an MCP client using one of the following transport options: HTTP transport (Recommended), SSE (Server-Sent Events), or stdio

evidence.title: AI SDK — MCP tools adapter
evidence.quote: The client's tools method acts as an adapter between MCP tools and AI SDK tools.

members.note: The agent loop IS the ReAct shape (reason → act → observe → repeat), but upstream docs never use the term 'ReAct'. Downgraded from first-class to supported because the pattern is implemented but not named.

evidence.title: AI SDK — Agents overview
evidence.quote: Agents are large language models (LLMs) that use tools in a loop to accomplish tasks.

evidence.title: AI SDK — Building agents (step shape)
evidence.quote: In each step, the model either generates text or calls a tool. If it generates text, the agent completes.

evidence.title: AI SDK — Building agents (loop)
evidence.quote: After each tool execution, the agent triggers a new generation where the model can call another tool or generate text.

members.note: Default stepCountIs(20); stopWhen accepts any predicate (stepCountIs, hasToolCall, custom).

evidence.title: AI SDK — Loop control default cap
evidence.quote: By default, agents stop after 20 steps using `stepCountIs(20)`.

evidence.title: AI SDK — Loop control safety framing
evidence.quote: This default is a safety measure to prevent runaway loops that could result in excessive API calls and costs.

evidence.title: AI SDK — stopWhen multi-step
evidence.quote: With the `stopWhen` setting, you can enable multi-step calls in `generateText` and `streamText`.

members.note: Streams accept AbortSignal; onAbort callback fires on abort (onFinish skipped). useChat exposes a stop() helper.

evidence.title: AI SDK — Error handling (abort)
evidence.quote: When streams are aborted (e.g., via chat stop button), you may want to perform cleanup operations like updating stored messages in your UI.

evidence.title: AI SDK — onAbort callback
evidence.quote: The `onAbort` callback is called when a stream is aborted via `AbortSignal`, but `onFinish` is not called.

evidence.title: AI SDK — useChat stop button
evidence.quote: When the user clicks the 'Stop' button, the fetch request will be aborted.

members.note: generateObject / streamObject accept Zod, Valibot, or JSON Schema; schemas drive both generation and validation. Composable with tool calling.

evidence.title: AI SDK — Generating structured data (schemas)
evidence.quote: You can use Zod schemas, Valibot, or JSON schemas to specify the shape of the data that you want.

evidence.title: AI SDK — Structured output + tool calling
evidence.quote: Structured output generation is part of the generateText and streamText flow. This means you can combine it with tool calling.

evidence.title: AI SDK — Schema validation
evidence.quote: The schema is also used to validate the generated data, ensuring type safety and correctness.

members.note: Tools are objects with description, inputSchema (Zod/JSON Schema), execute; passed to generateText / streamText via tools param.

evidence.title: AI SDK — Tool shape
evidence.quote: A tool consists of three properties: `description`, `inputSchema`, and `execute`.

evidence.title: AI SDK — Passing tools
evidence.quote: You can use tools with `generateText` and `streamText` by passing one or more tools to the `tools` parameter.

evidence.title: AI SDK — Schema dual-use
evidence.quote: A Zod schema or a JSON schema that defines the input parameters. The schema is consumed by the LLM, and also used to validate the LLM tool calls

anti_patterns_avoided.note: Provider abstraction is a stated design goal: a unified language-model spec lets callers swap providers without rewriting application code.

evidence.title: AI SDK — Providers framing
evidence.quote: Each provider typically has its own unique method for interfacing with their models, complicating the process of switching providers and increasing the risk of vendor lock-in.

alternatives.note: Same agent-sdk category; Vercel is TS-first multi-provider, Claude Agent SDK is Anthropic-only with built-in filesystem tools.
Both ship TS + Python flavours; OpenAI is Handoff-centric while Vercel is loop-centric.
Same category, different host language (Python/Java vs TypeScript).
TypeScript-native agent framework.
LangChain.js covers similar territory in TS.
Peer provider-neutral TS-first AI app framework.
Vercel's generateObject covers the structured-output slice in TypeScript with Zod schemas.

references.quote: The AI Toolkit for TypeScript. From the creators of Next.js, the AI SDK is a free open-source library for building AI-powered applications and agents

references.title: AI SDK — Introduction
references.quote: The TypeScript toolkit designed to help developers build AI-powered applications and agents

compositions.name: Strands Agents
compositions.intent: A model-driven approach to building AI agents in just a few lines of code.
compositions.description: Lightweight, model-driven agent SDK: define an agent with a model, system prompt and tools, and let the model drive the loop. Native MCP server support, 13+ model providers (Amazon Bedrock default, plus OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini/Ollama and others), multi-agent and autonomous-agent primitives, bidirectional audio streaming, Python-decorator tools and hot-reloadable tool directories. AWS-originated, now an open community project.
primary_use_cases: building agents in a few lines with a model-driven loop
MCP-native tool integration across many providers
multi-agent and autonomous-agent systems, including real-time audio
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Model-driven loop: the model decides which tools to call and when to stop, given a model, system prompt and a set of decorator-defined tools. Supports multi-agent composition and bidirectional streaming for audio.

key_concepts.name: Model-driven loop
key_concepts.summary: The model decides which tools to call and when to stop.

key_concepts.name: Tool decorators
key_concepts.summary: Python decorators register tools; tool directories hot-reload.

key_concepts.name: MCP
key_concepts.summary: Native Model Context Protocol server support.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD
  GOAL[Task] --> LOOP[Model-driven loop]
  LOOP --> DECIDE{Call tool?}
  DECIDE -->|yes| TOOL[Decorator tool / MCP tool]
  TOOL --> LOOP
  DECIDE -->|done| OUT[Result]
  LOOP -.multi-agent.-> WORKERS[Sub-agents]
  LOOP -.audio.-> STREAM[Bidirectional streaming]

members.note: Easily build tools using Python decorators

evidence.title: strands-agents/sdk-python README
evidence.quote: Easily build tools using Python decorators

evidence.quote: Model Agnostic: Support for Amazon Bedrock, Anthropic, Gemini, LiteLLM, Llama, Ollama, OpenAI, Writer, and custom providers

members.note: Advanced Capabilities: Multi-agent systems, autonomous agents, and streaming support
